desc: Slender, pale green-brown reduviid. Applies sticky resin from tibial glands to forelegs for prey capture — a 'flypaper trap.' Important garden predator of leafhoppers, caterpillars, and other pest insects. Can bite painfully in self-defense. Beneficial.

fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; sticky forelegs; captures flies, aphids

hp: Caterpillar predator. In gardens. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Large, dark assassin bug attracted to lights. Painful bite — one of the most painful insect bites in SoCal. Not a kissing bug.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flies to lights; painful defensive bite
fm.Habitat: Under bark, debris; enters homes at night

hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Reduviidae.

desc: North America's largest assassin bug. Distinctive cogwheel crest on thorax. Powerful predator. At western range edge in LA.

fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; slow; painful bite if handled
fm.Diet: Predator — caterpillars, beetles, other large insects

hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Reduviidae.

desc: Bloodsucking assassin bug. Vector of Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi). Bites sleeping humans at night. Found in woodrat nests.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with orange-red lateral margins
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood feeder; bites face/lips of sleeping hosts; defecates near bite (disease transmission)
fm.Habitat: Woodrat nests, rock crevices; enters homes

hp: Chagas disease vector — Trypanosoma cruzi confirmed in SoCal triatomines (Shender et al. 2016, PLoS NTD). Nocturnal blood-feeder.

desc: Spectacular orange-and-black pentatomid with bold checkered pattern. Major pest of Brassicaceae — cabbage, kale, broccoli, and mustard. Bright aposematic warning coloration advertises its foul taste and smell. Aggregates on plants. Year-round in SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious; sucks plant juices; warning coloration; bad smell
fm.Habitat: Gardens, crops — wherever cruciferous plants grow

hp: On brassicaceae (mustard family). Agricultural pest

desc: Invasive from Asia. Shield-shaped brown with marbled pattern. Major agricultural pest. Aggregates in homes in fall.

fm.Behavior: Invasive; aggregates in buildings fall/winter; agricultural pest

hp: Brown marmorated stink bug — invasive from Asia causing $37M+ annual crop damage in eastern US (Leskey & Nielsen 2018). Under surveillance in SoCal.

desc: Bright green shield bug common on vegetables and fruit. Piercing-sucking mouthparts damage tomatoes and peppers. Strong defensive odor.

fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; strong defensive smell; damages fruit

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Pentatomidae.

desc: Say's green stink bug — bright green pentatomid with diagnostic pale lateral margins. Named for Thomas Say, father of American entomology. Extremely common on native plants. Feeds on developing seeds and fruit. Can damage tomatoes and other crops when abundant.

fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; aggregates on seed heads

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts

desc: Large plant bug with flattened hind tibiae ('leaf feet'). White zigzag band across wings. Major citrus/pomegranate pest.

fm.Color: Dark brown; white zigzag band on wings; flattened hind legs
fm.Behavior: Plant piercer; damages fruit; gregarious nymphs; strong flier
fm.Diet: Fruit and seeds — citrus, pomegranate, tomato

hp: Western leaf-footed bug — on citrus, pomegranate, tomato, and almonds. Piercing-sucking damage to developing fruit. Buzzy flight.

desc: Western leaffooted bug — brown coreid with expanded hind tibiae. Feeds on developing seeds and fruit. Common on almonds, pistachios, tomatoes. Aggregates in fall.
hp: Solanaceae associate — feeds on tomato, pepper, or nightshade family plants. Coreidae.

desc: Thomas's leaf-footed bug — large dark coreid (20-25mm) on agave, yucca, and prickly pear cactus. Powerfully built hind femora with pronounced spines. Feeds on plant sap and developing fruit. Produces pungent defensive odor. Males fence with hind legs in territorial disputes. Desert and foothill habitats.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; robust; spiny hind femora
fm.Behavior: On agave flower stalks; slow; strong flier

hp: Cactus; agave; pine; yucca. Associated with Coastal Prickly Pear and Desert Agave.

desc: Dark pyrrhocorid with vivid orange border. Nymphs are spectacular: gregarious aggregations of metallic blue individuals. Striking at all life stages. On native plants. Feeds on plant sap. One of the most visually dramatic true bugs in LA County.

fm.Color: Adults: dark with orange margins. Nymphs: metallic blue (striking)
fm.Behavior: Gregarious; feeds on many plants; nymphs conspicuously blue

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Metallic coloring.

desc: Red-orange and black seed bug on milkweed. Sequesters cardiac glycosides from milkweed seeds. Warning coloration deters predators.

fm.Color: Red-orange and black; X-pattern on wings
fm.Behavior: Gregarious on milkweed; toxic; warning coloration

hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides. Sequesters cardia.

desc: Orange-banded on black. Classic aposematic (warning-colored) insect used extensively in biology research and education. Feeds on milkweed seeds, sequestering cardiac glycosides for chemical defense. Easy to rear — a standard teaching organism in entomology labs.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on milkweed seed pods; toxic

hp: On milkweed — sequesters cardiac glycosides

desc: Black with red-orange lines. Aggregates en masse on sun-warmed walls in fall, alarming homeowners with swarms of hundreds. Feeds on boxelder and maple seeds. Nuisance pest — enters buildings through gaps. Harmless. Produces foul odor if crushed.

fm.Behavior: Mass aggregations on warm walls; enters homes fall/winter

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Rhopalidae.

desc: Invasive pentatomid from Africa, first detected in SoCal in 2008. Small (5-7mm) but devastating — black, white, and orange shield shape. Feeds on all Brassicaceae — decimates broccoli, kale, arugula, and native mustards. Rapidly spreading. Major emerging pest.

fm.Color: Black with white/orange markings; shield-shaped; small
fm.Behavior: Invasive; pierces plant tissue causing severe damage; spreading rapidly

hp: On brassicaceae (mustard family). Agricultural pest

desc: Walks on water surface using hydrophobic leg hairs. Detects prey vibrations through water. Common on any slow stream or pond.

fm.Behavior: Skates on water surface film; detects vibrations; predator
fm.Habitat: Streams, ponds, any calm water surface

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Gerridae.

desc: Bizarre treehopper with tall, laterally compressed thorn-like pronotum. Tropical species established in urban SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on ornamental trees; females guard eggs

hp: On ornamental trees and shrubs. Thorn-mimicking sap feeder. Ant-tended for honeydew.

desc: Brilliant turquoise-blue with orange spots. One of the most photogenic insects in LA County. On coast live oak.

fm.Behavior: Gregarious on oak twigs; maternal care of eggs

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak)

desc: Large leafhopper. Vector of Pierce's disease — devastating to grapevines. Invasive from SE USA. Dark with clear wings.

fm.Behavior: Powerful flier; xylem feeder; excretes copious liquid; disease vector
fm.Diet: Xylem sap — polyphagous; vectors Xylella fastidiosa

hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines. On grape. Invasive.

desc: Vivid blue-green and red-orange striped leafhopper. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines, but a native species (unlike the invasive glassy-winged sharpshooter). One of the most colorful leafhoppers in NA. On many plant species.

fm.Behavior: Xylem feeder; vectors Pierce's disease; on riparian vegetation

hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines. On grape. Invasive.

desc: Red-banded leafhopper — spectacular leafhopper with parallel red and blue-green stripes — one of the most colorful insects in LA gardens. On ornamental plants. Can vector Pierce's disease of grapevines (Xylella fastidiosa). Despite beauty, a potential pest. Very common.

fm.Color: Bright red and blue-green parallel stripes
fm.Behavior: Hops readily when disturbed; common on ornamental shrubs

hp: Viticulture associate — on grapevines (Vitis). Vineyard pest or resident. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines.

desc: LA's most common large summer cicada. Males produce deafening continuous buzz from tree canopies on hot days — the soundtrack of SoCal summers. Desert-adapted — can thermoregulate by evaporative cooling. Nymphs develop underground 2-5 years feeding on tree roots.

fm.Sound: Loud continuous buzz on hot days (>95°F); can reach 90dB
fm.Behavior: Males sing from canopy; nymphs underground 2–5 years
fm.Habitat: Desert edge, urban trees — hot dry areas

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Large species. Cicadidae.

desc: Small, dark cicada of mountain chaparral. Males produce quiet clicking sounds (not the loud buzz of Diceroprocta). Often goes unnoticed because the song is subtle. Named for entomologist F.W. Putnam. Multi-year underground nymphal development on shrub roots.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; small for a cicada
fm.Behavior: Males click from branches; cryptic

hp: Root feeder on chaparral shrubs. Root-feeding nymph underground.

desc: Medium-sized dark cicada of foothill and mountain habitats. Males produce loud buzzy song from shrubs and trees. One of several Okanagana species.

fm.Behavior: Canopy singer; nymphs underground for years

hp: Root-feeding nymph on shrubs/trees for years underground. Adult male song from foothill chaparral.

desc: Mottled brown pentatomid. One of the most frequently encountered shield bugs in LA gardens. Agricultural pest of tomatoes, peppers, peaches, and almonds — piercing-sucking feeding causes dimpling and cat-facing damage on fruit. Overwinters in leaf litter.

fm.Color: Mottled brown with pale speckling; shield-shaped
fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; damages fruit; aggregates overwinter

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas. Consperse.

desc: Small, extremely variable mirid (brown, green, or reddish). One of the most economically important agricultural pests in western NA — damages cotton, alfalfa, strawberries, and many vegetables by puncturing developing fruit and flower buds. Difficult to control.

fm.Color: Variable — green, brown, reddish; V-mark on scutellum
fm.Behavior: Fast-moving; feeds on flower buds and developing fruit
fm.Diet: Flower buds, developing seeds — major crop pest

hp: On flowers. Part of flower-visiting community

desc: Dark assassin bug with red-orange abdomen. Ambushes bees and other pollinators on flowers. Sticky resinous forelegs.

fm.Behavior: Ambushes bees on flowers; sticky resinous forelegs
fm.Diet: Predator — bees, flies, other flower visitors

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Flower visitor.

desc: Blood-sucking conenose — reddish-brown triatomine with orange lateral margins. Nocturnal blood-feeder on mammals. Can vector Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi) — confirmed cases in SoCal, though transmission risk remains low. Bites cause allergic reactions in sensitized individuals. Desert edge.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood feeder; Chagas disease vector

hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Potential vector of Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi).

desc: Dark shield-shaped coreid and major pest of cucurbits. Eggs are distinctive — copper-colored, laid in neat rows on leaf undersides. Both nymphs and adults sap-feed, causing wilting ('Anasa wilt'). Overwinters as adult under debris. Very difficult to control in gardens.

fm.Behavior: Sucks plant juices; causes wilting; overwinters under debris
fm.Habitat: Gardens — wherever squash, pumpkin, cucumber grow

hp: Garden and crop pest. Herbivore on cultivated plants

desc: Red-eyed soapberry bug with black-and-red coloring. Gregarious — forms conspicuous aggregations on golden rain tree (Koelreuteria) seeds. Originally on native Sapindaceae, rapidly adapted to ornamental rain trees. Classic case of host-switching evolution in real time.

fm.Color: Black with red eyes and red shoulders/margins
fm.Behavior: Mass aggregations on fallen seeds; harmless; conspicuous
fm.Diet: Seeds of Sapindaceae — golden rain tree, balloon vine

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Rhopalidae.

desc: Lunate leaf-footed bug — large brown coreid of desert creosote scrub. Distinctive crescent-shaped (lunate) expansions on hind tibiae. Feeds on creosote bush (Larrea) fruit and stems. One of the more specialized LA coreids — tied to desert scrub habitat.

fm.Color: Dark brown; crescent-shaped hind tibial lobes
fm.Behavior: On creosote bush; slow-moving

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Creosote Bush. Desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Delicate green insect with golden eyes and lacy wings. Larvae ('aphid lions') are voracious predators. Commercially sold as biocontrol.

fm.Size: ½ in body; wings to 1 in
fm.Color: Bright green; golden eyes; delicate lacy wings
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights; larvae are 'aphid lions' — voracious predators

hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Predatory. Common.

desc: Green stink bug — small green pentatomid common on many plants. Feeds on developing seeds and fruit. Often confused with other green shield bugs — requires examination of ventral surface and genitalia for definitive ID. Part of the large and difficult Banasa genus.

fm.Color: Green with narrow pale margins; shield-shaped
fm.Behavior: Plant feeder; common on tree foliage

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Pentatomidae.

desc: Predatory stink bug — one of the few. Prominent shoulder spines. Commercially used for biocontrol of pest caterpillars.

fm.Color: Brown with dark speckles; prominent shoulder spines
fm.Behavior: Predator — impales caterpillars and beetle larvae with beak

hp: Biocontrol agent; pine; caterpillar predator. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Bizarre toad-shaped bug of stream and pond margins. Bumpy, warty texture and bulging compound eyes convincingly mimic a tiny toad. Jumps to catch small arthropod prey on muddy banks. One of the most morphologically unusual true bugs in LA County.

fm.Color: Mottled gray-brown; bumpy; toad-like; bulging eyes
fm.Behavior: Jumps like a toad to catch prey; sits on mud/sand near water
fm.Habitat: Stream margins, pond edges — on wet sand/mud

hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Mimicry specialist.

desc: Conchuela stink bug — dark brown pentatomid with distinctive orange or red border and connexivum. Significant agricultural pest of beans, tomatoes, and cotton. Distinguished from C. sayi by dark brown (not green) ground color with orange trim. Strong flier.

fm.Shape: Dark brown stink bug with orange/red border

hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares

desc: Tiny (2-3mm) predatory anthocorid. Voracious consumer of thrips, spider mites, and aphid eggs. One of the most important biological control agents in agriculture — commercially reared for greenhouse release. Can bite humans — surprisingly painful for such a tiny insect.

fm.Shape: Tiny predator — eats thrips, mites, aphid eggs

hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Predatory. Very small species.

desc: Wickham's stilt bug — delicate, extremely long-legged berytid on vegetation. Predatory — feeds on small insects, mites, and insect eggs. Important beneficial bug in gardens and crops. Slender build and slow deliberate movement. Named for early collector H.F. Wickham.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens.

desc: Massive aquatic predator reaching 65mm. Ambushes fish, frogs, and large aquatic invertebrates with powerful raptorial forelegs. Delivers painful bite — earned nickname 'toe-biter.' Attracted to lights at night ('electric light bug'). Strong flier between water bodies.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Aquatic. Large species.

desc: Light green reduviid lurking on flowers and foliage. Ambush predator of pollinators and other flower-visiting insects. Sticky forelegs trap prey. Important garden predator. Can deliver a painful bite if handled carelessly. Native beneficial insect.

fm.Shape: Pale green predatory bug lurking on flowers

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Bordered plant bug — large dark bug with orange-red border. Nymphs brilliant metallic blue. Gregarious on various plants. One of the most photogenic true bugs.

fm.Color: Dark with orange-red margins; nymphs metallic blue

hp: Sap feeder on various native plants

desc: Large (18-20mm) brown coreid with distinctive flattened hind tibiae ('leaf-footed'). Feeds on developing conifer seeds. Enters homes en masse in fall seeking overwintering shelter. Loud buzzy flight. Harmless but alarming when found indoors. Now invasive in Europe.

fm.Shape: Large brown bug with flattened hind legs

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Invasive.

desc: Opuntia bug — brown coreid feeding on prickly pear cactus pads. Causes yellowing, scarring, and stippling damage to Opuntia. Can weaken plants but rarely kills them. Both nymphs and adults feed on cactus. An important herbivore in cactus ecology — controls Opuntia density.

fm.Shape: Brown bug feeding on prickly pear pads

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). Ant associate.

desc: Brown water scorpion — stick-like aquatic bug with long rear breathing tube (siphon). Ambush predator — grabs tadpoles, small fish, and aquatic insects with raptorial forelegs. Not a scorpion — the siphon is a snorkel, not a stinger. In still ponds and slow streams.

fm.Habitat: Stick-like aquatic bug with long breathing tube

hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Aquatic. Slow-moving.

desc: Pacific ash plant bug — small green mirid causing leaf stippling on ash trees. Can cause significant cosmetic damage to ornamental ash (Fraxinus) in heavy infestations — leaves become speckled and may drop prematurely. Most damaging in warm years. Native.

fm.Shape: Small plant bug causing leaf stippling on ash trees

hp: Specialist on ash trees (Fraxinus) — causes leaf stippling damage to ornamental ashes.

desc: Elm seed bug — small dark seed bug recently established in SoCal from Europe (first NA record 2012). Aggregates in enormous numbers on buildings in fall seeking overwintering shelter. On sycamore and boxelder seeds. Nuisance pest in homes but harmless. Rapidly expanding range.
hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa)

desc: Four-humped stink bug — bark-colored pentatomid perfectly camouflaged on tree trunks. Mottled gray-brown with four small pronotal bumps. Feeds on tree sap. Virtually invisible against oak bark. One of the best-camouflaged bugs in LA — flattened body hugs bark contours.

fm.Color: Bark-brown — excellent camouflage on tree trunks

hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. Flattened body.

desc: Pale water boatman — small aquatic hemipteran swimming with oar-like hind legs. Unlike backswimmers, swims right-side-up. Herbivorous — feeds on algae scraped from pond bottoms. Important fish food — a key link in freshwater food webs. Can fly between water bodies.

fm.Habitat: Small aquatic bug swimming with oar-like legs

hp: Aquatic larvae — important fish food.

desc: Wavy backswimmer — aquatic hemipteran that swims upside-down using long hind legs as oars. Air bubble trapped on ventral surface provides buoyancy and oxygen. Voracious predator of mosquito larvae, tadpoles, and small fish. Can deliver a surprisingly painful bite — handle carefully.

fm.Shape: Swims upside down using long hind legs as oars

hp: Predator of mosquito larvae — natural mosquito biocontrol. Mosquito associate. Predatory.

desc: Extremely flattened aradid living under bark of dead trees. Feeds on fungal hyphae in dead wood — one of the few heteropteran families with a fungal diet. Bark-matching brown coloring. Body so flat it can hide in crevices thinner than a dime.

fm.Shape: Extremely flat bug living under bark

hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Flattened body.

desc: American ambush bug — tiny (8-12mm) cryptic bug hiding in flower heads. Catches insects many times its own size — including bees, butterflies, and wasps — with enormously powerful raptorial forelegs. One of the most remarkably strong predators relative to body size in the insect world.

fm.Behavior: Catches insects much larger than itself

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Very small species.

desc: Pacific brown lacewing — small brown neuropteran (Order Neuroptera, not Hemiptera despite being grouped here). Both adults and larvae eat aphids — important biological control. Delicate lace-like wings. Nocturnal. Distinguished from green lacewings by brown color and smaller size.

fm.Shape: Small brown neuropteran (not a true bug)

hp: Biological control agent — predator of aphids. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid.

desc: Beautiful cicada — small dark cicada of mountain pine-oak forests. Males sing from treetops with distinctive song pattern. Multi-year underground nymphal development feeding on tree roots. Named 'bella' (beautiful) for the subtle wing patterning. Mountain habitats above 4,000 ft.

fm.Habitat: Small dark cicada of mountain forests

hp: Root-feeding nymph underground on oak and pine roots for years. Adult male sings from mountain trees. Mountain forest cicada.

desc: Tiny tingid with extraordinarily ornate, lace-like wing venation. Feeds on sycamore leaf undersides causing white stippling damage visible from above. Can partially defoliate trees in severe years. The delicate wing lace pattern is remarkably beautiful under magnification.

fm.Behavior: Causes stippling damage; can defoliate

hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa)

desc: Common bed bug — obligate human blood-feeder. Extremely flattened body hides in mattress seams, headboard cracks, and furniture joints by day. Night feeder — attracted by body heat and CO2. Major global resurgence since early 2000s due to insecticide resistance. Does not transmit disease.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal blood-feeder; resurgent since 2000s

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Cimicidae.

desc: Predatory pentatomid with red-and-black coloring. Specialist predator of Colorado potato beetle larvae — grasps and sucks them dry. Important biological control agent. Bold aposematic coloring warns predators of its own chemical defenses.

fm.Shape: Predatory stink bug with red and black coloring

hp: Biocontrol agent; predatory.

desc: Sweetpotato whitefly — tiny sap-feeding pest with enormous agricultural impact. Vectors over 100 plant viruses including tomato yellow leaf curl. Develops resistance to insecticides rapidly. Biotype B (silverleaf whitefly) is especially damaging. Billions in annual crop losses worldwide.
hp: Solanaceae associate — feeds on tomato, pepper, or nightshade family plants. Ant associate.

desc: White waxy-coated scale insect on citrus, grapes, and ornamentals. Produces copious honeydew promoting sooty mold growth. Vectors grapevine leafroll virus. Controlled by the parasitoid wasp Leptomastix dactylopii — another California biocontrol success story.
hp: Citrus; biocontrol agent; parasitoid of wasps. On Citrus (citrus). Scale insect associate.

desc: Circular armored scale on citrus bark, leaves, and fruit. One of the most important citrus pests worldwide. Female permanently attached under waxy cover. Controlled by parasitoid wasp Aphytis melinus — a classic biological control success story in California agriculture.
hp: Citrus; biocontrol agent; parasitoid of wasps. On Citrus (citrus). Under bark.

desc: The largest aphid in North America at 6mm. On oak, sycamore, and pecan bark. Ant-tended — ants protect aphids in exchange for honeydew. When threatened, adults kick defensively with hind legs. Impressive size makes it visible without magnification — unusual for an aphid.

fm.Size: ⅛ in–¼ in — largest NA aphid

hp: On Platanus racemosa (western sycamore). Causes leaf stippling. Ant-tended — mutualistic honeydew exchange.

desc: Bright yellow aphid on milkweed and oleander. Sequesters cardiac glycosides — toxic to predators. Cosmopolitan.
hp: Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides. Associated with Narrow-leaf Milkweed.

desc: Soft brown hemispherical scale on citrus. Major pest producing copious honeydew that promotes black sooty mold on leaves and fruit. Important target for biological control using parasitoid wasps (Metaphycus). Part of LA's significant citrus pest complex.
hp: Citrus; biocontrol agent; parasitoid of wasps. On Citrus (citrus). Scale insect associate.

desc: White cottony mass on Opuntia cactus hiding a tiny hemipteran that produces brilliant carmine red dye. Historically one of the most valuable trade commodities in the Americas — worth more than gold by weight. Still used in food coloring and cosmetics (E120).

fm.Color: Dark red body under white cochineal wax

hp: Leaf miner on host plant

desc: Small green aphid — most important plant virus vector worldwide. On peach (primary) and many crops (secondary).
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On peach.

desc: White cottony scale on citrus. Australian invasive that triggered first classical biocontrol success (Vedalia beetle 1889).

fm.Size: ⅛ in–⅓ in with white egg sac
fm.Color: Orange-brown body + white fluted egg mass

hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. Invasive.

desc: Tiny mottled brown psyllid. Vectors huanglongbing (citrus greening disease, HLB) — the most devastating citrus disease globally, with no cure. Infected trees produce bitter, misshapen fruit and die within years. CDFA quarantine pest under active eradication.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Sweet Orange.

desc: Tiny yellow thysanopteran that scars citrus fruit rind during the expanding phase, creating ring-like cosmetic damage that downgrades fresh-market fruit. California's most economically significant citrus pest. Rasping mouthparts feed on epidermal cells. Multiple generations per year.
hp: Citrus thrips — rasps citrus fruit rind during growth, causing ring-scarring. Most important citrus pest in CA (Morse & Hoddle 2006).

desc: Tiny white-winged sap-feeder. Clouds of adults fly up when plants are disturbed. Major greenhouse and garden pest worldwide. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Develops insecticide resistance rapidly. Controlled by parasitoid Encarsia formosa.
hp: Predator or parasitoid of whiteflies — biocontrol of crop pests.

desc: Veiny barklouse — small brown psocid living in herds on tree bark. Not a true louse — feeds on lichens, algae, and bark microflora. Harmless to trees and humans. Often found in groups of 20-50 on oak trunks. The gregarious herding behavior is distinctive and sometimes alarming to homeowners.

fm.Habitat: Tree bark — herds visible on trunks

hp: Oak; lichen. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Eastern boxelder bug — black with three red lines on pronotum. Aggregates by the thousands on warm sun-facing walls in fall and spring. Feeds on boxelder (Acer negundo) seeds. Nuisance pest in homes but completely harmless. Occasionally reaches SoCal from eastern populations.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter. Rhopalidae.

desc: Van Duzee's cicada — small dark cicada of inland valleys and oak woodland. Males sing from shrubs and trees. Multi-year underground development (4-7 years as root-feeding nymph). Named for E.P. Van Duzee, prolific hemipterist. Part of the endemic western cicada fauna.
hp: On oak roots underground. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Delicate green insect with golden eyes (technically Neuroptera). Larvae voracious aphid lions. Sold as biocontrol.
hp: Biological control agent — predator of aphids. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid.

desc: Short-horned antlion — adult is a delicate lacewing-like insect. Larva is the famous 'doodlebug' that digs conical sand pit traps to capture ants — one of the most remarkable predation strategies in the insect world. Desert and sandy habitats. Adults nocturnal, attracted to lights.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. Desert specialist.

desc: Tiny (1-2mm) slender thysanopteran. One of the most damaging agricultural pests worldwide — feeds on flowers and fruit, vectors tomato spotted wilt virus. Rasping-sucking mouthparts cause silvery scarring. Extremely difficult to control. Develops insecticide resistance rapidly.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers

desc: Invasive from Asia — not yet established in CA but under active quarantine. Adults spectacular: gray forewings with black spots, red hindwings. Report sightings to CDFA.

fm.Color: Gray FW with black spots; red HW
fm.Status: QUARANTINE PEST — report sightings to CDFA

hp: Spotted lanternfly — quarantine pest. Not established in CA. Feeds on 70+ hosts including grape (Dara et al. 2015). Active CDFA surveillance.

desc: Green thorn-shaped treehopper — diagnostic triangular pronotum. Feeds on plant sap. Female cuts crescent-shaped egg slits in bark. Common on alfalfa, weeds, and ornamentals.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community

desc: Invasive Australian psyllid devastating eucalyptus throughout LA since 1998. Nymphs build conspicuous white crystalline 'lerps' (sugar shelters) on leaves. Can defoliate trees.

fm.Habitat: Eucalyptus — white crystalline lerps on leaves diagnostic
fm.Damage: Defoliates eucalyptus; honeydew and sooty mold

hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Invasive Australian psyllid devastating eucalyptus throughou.

desc: Slender tan predatory bug. Important biological control agent — feeds on aphids, leafhoppers, caterpillars, and mites in agricultural fields. Resembles a thin assassin bug.

fm.Role: Biological control — feeds on aphids, leafhoppers, caterpillars

hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Predatory. Western.

desc: Invasive Mexican whitefly — produces spectacular long white waxy filaments hanging from leaf undersides. Common on hibiscus, banana, mulberry. Arrived in LA ~1992.

fm.Size: ⅛ in body; filaments up to 2 in
fm.Color: White; produces long waxy filaments hanging from leaves
fm.Habitat: Hibiscus, banana, mulberry — conspicuous white filaments

hp: On ornamentals — ficus, hibiscus, citrus. Produces long waxy filaments and copious honeydew.

desc: Bright yellow-green with 4 black stripes. Feeds on over 250 plant species. Causes distinctive round necrotic spots on leaves. Nymph is bright red.

fm.Damage: Round necrotic leaf spots at feeding sites

hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Ant associate.

desc: Bright green sharpshooter with pointed head. Vectors Pierce's disease of grapevines (Xylella fastidiosa). Common in irrigated areas, lawns, and riparian zones.

fm.Habitat: Irrigated lawns, riparian — common vector of Xylella

hp: Riparian corridor habitats.

desc: Small waxy-white planthopper. Nymphs produce conspicuous white waxy filaments. Polyphagous on citrus, ornamentals. Introduced from eastern US.

fm.Habitat: Citrus, ornamentals — nymphs produce white wax

hp: On Asteraceae (composites) — daisies, sunflowers, sagebrush

desc: Shiny black aphid on citrus new growth. Vectors Citrus Tristeza Virus. Dense colonies on tender shoots cause leaf curling. Managed by parasitoid wasps and lady beetles.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. Parasitoid.

desc: Small brown froghopper — nymphs produce distinctive white frothy 'spittle' masses on plant stems. The familiar foamy blobs on garden plants. Adults jump powerfully.

fm.Color: Variable — tan, brown, or black; mottled
fm.Habitat: Garden plants — nymphal spittle masses on stems diagnostic
fm.Behavior: Nymphs produce protective foam; adults jump far

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas. Western.

desc: One of the largest true bugs in North America — up to 1.5 in. Beautiful: dark with orange-red margins. Nymphs are brilliant metallic blue-green. Desert species on mesquite.

fm.Color: Dark with orange-red margins; nymphs metallic blue-green

hp: On mesquite (prosopis) in desert habitat. Metallic coloring (5 in).

desc: Thread-legged assassin bug — one of the most bizarre insects in LA County. Extremely slender, stick-like body. Steals prey from spider webs — enters web without triggering spider, plucks trapped insects free. Fragile appearance belies effective predatory lifestyle. Nocturnal.
hp: Builds web to capture insects and spiders.

desc: Colonial aphid producing white woolly wax. Dense colonies on apple, pyracantha, elm. Cottony masses diagnostic.
hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. On apple.

desc: Bright orange-red assassin bug. Sticky legs trap prey. Important garden predator. Common on milkweed and throughout gardens.
hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides.

desc: Tiny bug with intricate lace-like wings. Feeds on undersides of sycamore and alder leaves. Beautiful under magnification.
hp: On Platanus racemosa (western sycamore). Causes leaf stippling.

desc: Small green-yellow aphid on cotton, cucurbits, and ornamentals. Vectors many plant viruses. One of the most polyphagous aphids.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On cotton.

desc: Large green or pink aphid on roses. Very common garden pest. Dense colonies on buds and new growth. Managed by ladybugs and lacewings.
hp: Rose aphid — colonial sap-feeder on Rosa. Produces honeydew. Ant-tended.

desc: Handsome planthopper — small pale green flatid planthopper. Sits motionless on stems in wedge-shaped resting posture. Common on many ornamental and native plants. Produces small flocculent waxy filaments. Distinguished from similar planthoppers by the clean pale green coloring.
hp: Sap feeder on various plants. Wedge-shaped, leaf-mimicking. Produces flocculent waxy filaments.

desc: Small dark treehopper with pronotal horn. Lives in species complex — different host-plant races. Vibrational communication through stems.

fm.Color: Dark with pale markings; pronotal horn

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Membracidae.

desc: Striking red-and-black seed bug recently established in SoCal (~2009). Forms dense aggregations on malvaceous plants. Feeds on seeds. Expanding range.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Pyrrhocoridae.

desc: Bright green shield bug. One of the world's most important crop pests. Strong defensive odor. Feeds on over 30 crop families. Recently arrived in LA.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Ant associate.

desc: Bright green planthopper with pointed head. Introduced from Australia. Wedge-shaped, flattened. Jumps powerfully. Common on ornamentals and citrus.
hp: Torpedo bug — pale green planthopper on citrus and ornamentals. Introduced from Australia. Distinctive torpedo-shaped body. Honeydew producer.

desc: Large aquatic predator. Males carry eggs on back (paternal care — rare in insects). Catches fish, tadpoles, aquatic insects. Painful bite if handled.

fm.Behavior: ♂ carries eggs on back — paternal care

hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Aquatic. Large species.

desc: Common green plant bug. Named for the broken crossband on pronotum. Feeds on many plants. Very common in gardens and agricultural fields.

fm.Color: Green with broken pale band on pronotum

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Miridae

desc: Large red-and-black assassin bug specializing on bees. Ambushes pollinators on flowers using sticky resin on forelegs. Native and ecologically important.

fm.Behavior: Ambushes bees on flowers with sticky legs

hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Flower visitor.

desc: Green plant bug with dark punctures on pronotum. Introduced from Europe. Common pest of potatoes, legumes, and many garden plants.
hp: Garden and crop pest. Herbivore on cultivated plants

desc: Small pale rhopalid bug on grasses and weedy plants. Very common in disturbed habitats. Congregates on drying weedy vegetation in summer.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Rhopalidae.

desc: Gray-green waxy aphid forming dense colonies on brassica crops. Covered in powdery grayish wax (unlike green peach aphid). Major cole crop pest.

fm.Habitat: Cabbage, broccoli, kale — dense waxy colonies

hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On cabbage.

desc: Tiny moth-like insect on undersides of oak leaves. Nymphs produce distinctive crown-shaped waxy fringe. Common on coast live oak.

fm.Color: White; moth-like; crown-shaped nymphal wax on oak

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Very small species.

desc: Four-spined assassin bug — pale green assassin bug with four pronotal spines. Important predator of pest insects in gardens and agricultural fields. Can bite if handled.
hp: Four-spined assassin bug — predator in pine woodland and gardens. Captures caterpillars, beetles, and other insects. Beneficial predator.

desc: Cluster stink bug — introduced African stink bug recently established in SoCal (~2013). Yellow-orange with black punctures. Aggregates on various plants. Expanding range.
hp: Invasive from Africa (~2013). On Asteraceae and various crops

desc: Large dark neuropteran (dobsonfly relative). Larvae aquatic in streams. Adults attracted to lights near water. Long filiform antennae (vs. pectinate in Corydalus).
hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Corydalidae.

desc: Sage leafhopper — tiny cicadellid feeding on sage, rosemary, and other Lamiaceae. Causes white stippling damage. Introduced from Mediterranean. Pest of herb gardens.

fm.Damage: White stippling on sage and rosemary leaves

hp: On Rosa (roses) — garden and wild species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Manzanita gall aphid — tiny aphid inducing distinctive red-purple leaf-fold galls on manzanita (Arctostaphylos). Very common in chaparral. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark; galls are red-purple leaf folds on manzanita

hp: Native shrub specialist. Associated with Bigberry Manzanita.

desc: Small dark seed bug on milkweed. Similar to large milkweed bug but smaller and darker. Feeds on milkweed seeds in pods.
hp: On Asclepias (milkweed). Aposematic — sequesters cardiac glycosides.

desc: Brilliant plant bug — small attractive mirid with metallic blue-green sheen. Predatory — feeds on mites and small insects on plants. Beneficial.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Metallic coloring.

desc: Expanded plant bug — small pale mirid on various plants. Common in gardens and weedy areas. Part of the very diverse plant bug family (Miridae).
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Miridae.

desc: Tiny psyllid specialist on California pepper tree (Schinus molle). Forms pit galls on leaves. Very common wherever pepper trees grow.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on pepper tree leaves

hp: Gall-inducer on host plant

desc: Whitecrossed seed bug — dark seed bug with white X-pattern on back. Common on ragwort and other Asteraceae. Conspicuous aggregations on host plants.

fm.Color: Dark with white cross on back

hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. Associated with Composites.

desc: Tiny cryptic predator hiding in flower heads. Raptorial forelegs grab prey many times its size — catches butterflies, bees, even wasps.

fm.Behavior: Catches prey much larger than itself

hp: Flower-ambush predator — captures pollinating insects on flower heads.

desc: Small green leafhopper with distinctive spotted pattern. On various trees and shrubs. One of the more recognizable leafhoppers.
hp: On various trees. Thirteen-spotted pattern distinctive. Part of canopy leafhopper community.

desc: Adventive psyllid — introduced psyllid on various ornamental plants. Forms galls on new growth. Part of the growing guild of invasive psyllids in SoCal.

fm.Color: Pale green; galls on new growth

hp: Gall-inducing species.

desc: Tiny green leafhopper with two dark spots. Introduced from Asia. Pest of many ornamental plants. Vectors phytoplasma diseases.

fm.Color: Pale green with two dark spots

hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Agricultural/garden pest.

desc: Tiny dark leafhopper on woody plants. Named for its protruding head. Part of the diverse leafhopper fauna.

fm.Color: Dark; resembles a fly speck on bark

hp: On woody plants — part of the chaparral leafhopper community. Potential phytoplasma vector.

desc: Saturn seed bug — distinctive seed bug with pale ring markings. On various plants. Mediterranean introduction. Spreading in SoCal.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. Lygaeidae.

desc: Tiny psyllid on eucalyptus producing white crystalline 'lerps' (waxy covers). Introduced from Australia. Very common on eucalyptus.

fm.Color: Tiny; white crystalline lerps on eucalyptus

hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Introduced species. Very small species.

desc: Dark plant bug — small dark mirid on various plants. Very common but overlooked. Part of the hyperdiverse plant bug family.
hp: On various native and ornamental plants

desc: Small green leafhopper on privet and olive. Vectors phytoplasma diseases. Very common on ornamental privet hedges.
hp: Privet leafhopper — vectors phytoplasma diseases to many ornamental and crop plants. On privet, ornamentals. Important disease vector.

desc: Small dark bug with translucent wings. Introduced from Old World. Pest of cotton and malvaceous plants. Recently established in SoCal.
hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares. Introduced species.

desc: Orange-brown assassin bug with spiny pronotum. Predatory on garden insects. Common on vegetation. Can bite if handled.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Spiny/armed.

desc: Four-spotted leafhopper — small green leafhopper with four dark spots on wings. On various plants in gardens and chaparral.

fm.Color: Green with four dark wing spots

hp: Chaparral sap feeder — on native shrubs. Part of native plant-insect community. Family Cicadellidae. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Smoke tree sharpshooter — large leafhopper with mottled wings. Native. Similar to glassy-winged sharpshooter. On smoke tree and other desert plants.

fm.Color: Mottled brown; large for a leafhopper

hp: Desert and desert-edge habitats. Desert specialist. Large species.

desc: Margined plant bug — small mirid with dark margins. On various plants in chaparral and gardens. Plant sap feeder.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Chaparral. Plant sap feeder.

desc: Striking red-and-black assassin bug. Predatory — catches caterpillars and other insects. Common in chaparral. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
hp: Caterpillar predator. Chaparral. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Small green-yellow leafhopper on oaks and other hardwood trees. Common in woodland canopy. Feeds on mesophyll cell contents. Part of the extremely diverse canopy leafhopper community — individual oak trees may host 20+ leafhopper species.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Consort.

desc: Corn leaf aphid — small dark aphid colonizing corn and grain leaves. Can build enormous colonies. Important crop pest. Honeydew attracts sooty mold.

fm.Color: Dark blue-green; colonial on corn/grain leaves

hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. On corn.

desc: Black-striped planthopper — small green planthopper with dark stripes. On grasses and sedges. Jumps powerfully when disturbed.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Delphacidae.

desc: Small pear-shaped soft scale on various ornamentals. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold. Can damage landscape plants.
hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. Scale insect associate.

desc: Small psocid on tree bark and dead branches. Feeds on fungi and algae. Forms groups. Harmless.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Under bark.

desc: Small black froghopper with red-orange markings. Nymphs produce frothy spittle on grass stems. Named for fire-like coloring. Native.
hp: Citrus associate — on orange, lemon, grapefruit (Rutaceae). Cercopidae.

desc: California pepper tree psyllid — tiny psyllid inducing pit galls on California pepper tree (Schinus molle) leaves. Very common wherever pepper trees grow in LA.

fm.Color: Tiny; pit galls on pepper tree leaves

hp: Specialist on California pepper tree — induces pit galls on leaf tissue. Native psyllid.

desc: Small pale green mirid. Pest of cotton — feeds on developing flower buds causing square loss. Common on various weedy plants.
hp: On flowers. Part of flower-visiting community

desc: Cactus coreid — leaf-footed bug specializing on prickly pear cactus fruit. Flattened hind tibiae. Common wherever Opuntia grows. Native.
hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). Flattened body.

desc: Large green or pink aphid on potatoes, tomatoes, and many plants. Long cornicles. Vectors many plant viruses. Very common garden pest.

fm.Color: Green or pink; long dark cornicles

hp: Corn (Zea mays) associate — larvae feed on roots, silk, or kernels

desc: Brown leaf-footed bug on various plants. Expanded hind tibiae. Common on prickly pear and other plants in foothill habitats.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Coastal Prickly Pear. Tip-marked.

desc: Tiny bug with intricate lace-patterned wings. Feeds on sycamore leaf undersides. Causes stippling damage. Beautiful under magnification.
hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa)

desc: Green sharpshooter with two spiny projections on vertex. Vectors Xylella. Common on oaks and riparian plants.
hp: Plant disease vector — transmits pathogens via piercing-sucking feeding. Associated with Oak.

desc: Colorful leafhopper with red and green banding. Common on ornamentals. One of several Graphocephala sharpshooters in LA.
hp: On ornamentals. Potential vector of Xylella fastidiosa (Pierce's disease) to grapevines.

desc: Green sharpshooter found on willows along riparian corridors. Similar to other Graphocephala but host-specific.
hp: Leafhopper — xylem/phloem sap feeder. Part of canopy insect community. Associated with Willow.

desc: Dark gray seed bug common in weedy areas. Aggregates in large numbers. Feeds on seeds of various plants. Often found under debris.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Lygaeidae.

desc: Green shield bug similar to green stink bug but with white-tipped spine on each shoulder. Common on various plants. Defensive stink odor.
hp: In conifer forests (pinus). Associated with Coulter Pine

desc: Medium-dark cicada of oak woodland. Males produce loud buzzing song. Common in foothill canyons. Named for hemipterist E.P. Van Duzee.

fm.Color: Dark; loud buzzing song in oaks

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Oak woodland habitat. Van.

desc: Medium green leafhopper. Vectors Pierce's disease of grapevines. Common on many ornamental and native plants.
hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Vectors Pierce's disease (Xylella fastidiosa) to grapevines. On grape. Cicadellidae.

desc: Soft white waxy insect with very long waxy tail filaments (longer than body). Pest of citrus, grape, and ornamentals. Ants tend it for honeydew.

fm.Color: White waxy; tail filaments longer than body

hp: Sap feeder on citrus — honeydew producer.

desc: Waxy scale insect resembling a small barnacle. On many ornamental and citrus plants. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
hp: Barnacle wax scale — hemispherical scale on citrus, holly, and ornamentals. Waxy coating resembles barnacle. Honeydew promotes sooty mold.

desc: Extremely slender thread-like bug. Predator of mites and small insects on vegetation. So thin it's nearly invisible in profile.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Mute.

desc: Small psocid on tree bark with distinctive F-shaped wing markings. Feeds on algae and lichens. Very common on tree trunks. Gregarious.

fm.Color: Pale with F-pattern wings; on tree bark

hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Under bark. F-winged.

desc: Large green aphid forming colonies on lupine stems and flower buds. Long cornicles. Can damage ornamental and native lupines.

fm.Color: Green; long black cornicles; on lupine

hp: Aphid — colonial sap-feeder on Lupinus (lupine) stems. Honeydew attracts ants.

desc: Cicada associated with Santa Catalina Island and mainland coastal scrub. Males sing from shrubs. Part of California's diverse cicada fauna.
hp: Cicada hunter — catches cicadas in flight for larval provisions.

desc: Tiny wingless psocid found indoors in damp areas. Feeds on mold and starchy materials. Can infest old books and papers.

fm.Color: Pale; wingless; in damp indoor areas

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Tiny. Feeds on mold and starchy materials.

desc: California plant bug — small green mirid on phacelia and other wildflowers. Native. Part of the enormous and diverse plant bug family.
hp: On flowers. Part of flower-visiting community. Ant associate.

desc: Tiny predatory bug with enormous eyes. Important biocontrol agent — feeds on aphids, whiteflies, mites, and insect eggs in crops.
hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Predatory. Very small species.

desc: Tiny moth-like insect producing dense woolly wax on citrus leaf undersides. Introduced from South America. Managed by parasitoid wasps.

fm.Color: White; woolly waxy tufts on citrus

hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. Managed by parasitoid wasps.

desc: Tiny flat bug from Australia damaging eucalyptus. Causes leaf bronzing and defoliation. Invasive pest spreading through California since ~2016.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Invasive.

desc: Pale seed bug specializing on sycamore seeds. Can invade homes in large numbers in fall when sycamores drop seeds. Harmless but alarming in quantity.

fm.Behavior: Mass home invasions in fall

hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa)

desc: Dark assassin bug with spiny body surface. Predator of other insects. Found in leaf litter and under debris. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
hp: Predator in leaf litter — hunts small arthropods. Spiny/armed.

desc: Tiny psyllid producing copious white waxy secretion on blue gum eucalyptus. Introduced from Australia. Can defoliate young trees.

fm.Color: Tiny; white wax on eucalyptus shoots

hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Introduced species. Very small species.

desc: Tiny pale mirid. Major pest of cotton — damages developing squares. Also on many weedy plants. Jumps actively.
hp: Cotton pest — feeds on cotton bolls, leaves, or squares. Miridae.

desc: Tiny whitefly on undersides of native tree leaves. Named for Stanford University collections. Produces waxy secretion.
hp: On undersides of native tree leaves. Part of the diverse native California whitefly community.

desc: Small pale green plant bug feeding on tobacco, tomato, and other Solanaceae. Common garden pest. Causes stippling damage to leaves.
hp: Garden and crop pest. Herbivore on cultivated plants

desc: Dark seed bug with reddish wing membrane. Under bark and in leaf litter. Feeds on seeds. Native.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Under bark.

desc: Small green-brown mirid found on tomato plants. Both pest (feeds on plant) and predator (eats whitefly eggs). Complex ecological role.
hp: Solanaceae associate — feeds on tomato, pepper, or nightshade family plants. Predatory.

desc: Small green mirid associated with yerba santa (Eriodictyon) in chaparral. Host-specific. Part of diverse California native mirid fauna.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Thick-leaved Yerba Santa.

desc: Tiny bug with intricate lace-patterned wings on various shrubs. Causes stippling damage to leaves. Beautiful under magnification.
hp: On various native shrubs — causes stippling damage. Intricate lace-wing venation.

desc: Small predatory mirid feeding on mites, thrips, and small insects. Beneficial in gardens. Originally European.
hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on thrips.

desc: Tiny but fierce predator. Important biocontrol of thrips in strawberry and other crops. Can deliver surprisingly painful bite relative to tiny size.
hp: Minute pirate bug — tiny (2mm) but fierce predator of thrips, mites, and aphid eggs. One of the most important biocontrol agents in CA crops.

desc: Small brown leafhopper with pale crossband. Common on various shrubs in chaparral and gardens.
hp: Chaparral sap feeder — on native shrubs. Part of native plant-insect community. Family Cicadellidae. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Small psocid on tree bark. Feeds on algae, lichen, and fungi. Gregarious — often in groups. Harmless. Common on native oaks.
hp: Bark microflora feeder — on lichens, algae, and fungal spores on tree bark. On Quercus (oak). Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Tiny psyllid on Acacia and Albizia. Introduced from Australia. Forms dense colonies producing honeydew. Common in urban plantings.
hp: On Acacia and Albizia. Introduced from Australia with host plantings. Dense colony former.

desc: White woolly aphid introduced from Asia. Forms dense colonies on hackberry (Celtis) leaves. Produces copious honeydew.

fm.Color: White waxy coating on hackberry leaves

hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. Preys on aphid. Introduced species.

desc: Small green leafhopper with six dark spots on vertex. On oak and other hardwoods. Named for entomologist McAtee.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Family Cicadellidae. Six-spotted.

desc: Cherubic gall aphid — aphid inducing swollen petiole galls on poplar leaves. Complex life cycle alternating between poplar (primary) and plant roots (secondary).
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Family Aphididae.

desc: Tiny aphid inducing red leaf-edge galls on manzanita. Related to T. coweni. Common in chaparral wherever manzanita grows.

fm.Color: Dark; red leaf-edge galls on manzanita

hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. Tiny

desc: Green citrus aphid — bright green aphid on citrus and spiraea. One of the most common aphid pests worldwide. Vectors citrus tristeza virus.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. Aphid predator.

desc: Brown shield bug similar to Euschistus but smaller. On various plants. Less common than other stink bugs.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Pentatomidae.

desc: Tiny mirid with distinctive blue-tinted eyes. On various native shrubs in chaparral. Attractive under magnification.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. In chaparral habitat. Very small species.

desc: Dark seed bug with reddish-orange collar behind head. Feeds on seeds of various plants. Common in weedy areas and under debris.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Lygaeidae.

desc: Small green aphid on mint family plants (Lamiaceae). Introduced from Mediterranean. Common on garden herbs.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue

desc: Small dark rhyparochromid found under debris and in leaf litter. Feeds on seeds. Common but secretive ground-dwelling species.
hp: Family Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Small pale leafhopper. Common but overlooked due to small size. On various grasses and herbs.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Cicadellidae.

desc: Small psocid on tree bark feeding on algae, fungi, and lichens. Gregarious — forms groups on bark surfaces. Harmless.
hp: Bark microflora feeder — on lichens, algae, and fungal spores on tree bark. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Green shield bug with distinctive reddish-orange shoulder stripe. On many plants including vegetables. Can damage tomatoes and berries.
hp: On many plants — generalist. Red-orange shoulder stripe distinguishes from other green stink bugs.

desc: Western red-shouldered stink bug — closely related to T. pallidovirens. Green with reddish shoulder band. On agricultural crops. Part of a difficult species complex.
hp: On vegetables, fruit, and native plants. Piercing-sucking damage to developing seeds and fruit.

desc: Tiny psyllid vectoring zebra chip disease of potato (Candidatus Liberibacter). Major quarantine concern. Also on tomato and pepper.

fm.Warning: Vectors zebra chip disease — quarantine pest

hp: Potato/tomato psyllid — vectors zebra chip disease (Candidatus Liberibacter solanacearum) devastating potatoes. Major emerging crop pest.

desc: Tiny tingid with delicate wing venation. On various plants. Wing pattern resembles fine lacework — beautiful under magnification.
hp: On various shrubs — leaf underside feeder causing stippling. Delicate lace-patterned wings.

desc: Small scale insect producing distinctive waxy secretion on oak bark. Native. Part of the diverse sap-feeding insect community on California oaks.
hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. Under bark.

desc: Tiny psyllid with dark wing spots. On various plants. Part of California's diverse psyllid fauna.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Spotted-winged.

desc: Slender thread-legged bug with hairy legs. Remarkable — enters spider webs to steal prey or eat the spider itself. Kleptoparasite.

fm.Behavior: Enters spider webs — steals prey or eats spider

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Parasitoid.

desc: Tiny psyllid on tree mallow (Malva). Introduced from Mediterranean. Can damage ornamental mallows. Waxy secretion on leaves.
hp: Introduced species. Associated with Cheeseweed.

desc: Small brown treehopper with elongated pronotal horn. On oak twigs. Part of diverse treehopper community on California oaks.

fm.Color: Brown; pronotal horn; on oak twigs

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Van.

desc: Extremely slender assassin bug. Predatory on small insects in spider webs and bark crevices. So thin it's nearly invisible in profile.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Spider specialist. Under bark.

desc: Soft scale heavily infesting ice plant (Carpobrotus). Produces white cottony egg sacs. Can damage ornamental and invasive ice plant groundcover.

fm.Color: Brown with white cottony egg sac; on ice plant

hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. On cotton.

desc: Small dark lygaeid found in leaf litter and under debris. Seed-feeding. Named for entomologist Wright. Part of diverse ground-dwelling bug community.
hp: Family Lygaeidae.

desc: Extremely slender stick-like assassin bug. Thread-thin legs and body. Predator in vegetation. Among the most bizarre-looking LA County insects.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Arizona.

desc: Nigra scale — dark soft scale on ornamental plants and citrus. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold. Managed by parasitoid wasps and ladybugs.
hp: Nigra scale — black hemispherical scale on citrus and ornamentals. Managed by parasitoid wasps (Coccophagus, Metaphycus). Honeydew producer.

desc: Smooth dark dome-shaped soft scale on many ornamental plants. Produces honeydew. Named for occurrence on coffee plants. Pantropical.
hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Scale insect associate.

desc: Tiny delphacid planthopper on grasses and sedges. One of many small planthoppers in grassland and marsh habitats.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Marsh/wetland.

desc: Tiny psyllid on eucalyptus growing tips. Introduced from Australia. Produces white lerp (waxy cover). Damages new growth.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Tiny. Introduced from Aus.

desc: Small psyllid on various native shrubs. Part of the diverse native psyllid fauna of California chaparral.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. In chaparral habitat. Bristly.

desc: Tiny psyllid on Ficus species. Introduced from Asia. Causes leaf curling and sooty mold on ornamental figs.
hp: Introduced species. Associated with Common Fig.

desc: Small green mirid on various plants. Named for pioneer West Coast hemipterist E.P. Van Duzee.

desc: Tiny psyllid on Acacia baileyana (Cootamundra wattle). Introduced from Australia with its host.
