desc: Tiny aphid on alder leaves. Introduced from Europe. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
hp: On alder (alnus) in riparian habitat. Associated with White Alder. Introduced species.

desc: Green aphid on arbutus (strawberry tree). Introduced from Mediterranean. On ornamental plantings.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On strawberr.

desc: American cochineal — scale insect producing carmine red dye. On prickly pear cactus. Historical source of red pigment for textiles and food coloring.

fm.Color: White waxy coating; crushed = brilliant red dye
fm.Note: Source of carmine red dye — historic

hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). Scale insect associate.

desc: Tiny predatory anthocorid with spiny body. Feeds on thrips and mites. Beneficial predator in gardens.
hp: Predatory — hunts arthropod prey. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on thrips.

desc: Intermediate plant bug — small pale mirid on various plants. Feeds on plant sap. Part of the staggeringly diverse Miridae — the largest heteropteran family. Many mirid species await description from California. Requires microscopic examination of male genitalia for firm ID.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Ant associate.

desc: Tiny psyllid on mesquite trees. Native. Can cause leaf distortion. Part of desert plant-insect community.
hp: On mesquite (prosopis) in desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Medium leafhopper with elongated body. On various trees. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community.
hp: On various trees. Large-bodied canopy leafhopper. Part of the diverse woodland canopy community.

desc: Tiny coccinellid that preys on scale insects. Classified in both Coccinellidae and historically associated with Hemiptera due to its prey.
hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect. Scale insect associate.

desc: Small mirid on various plants. Named from Greek synecdoche (literary device) — the common name is a literal translation.
hp: On various plants

desc: Tiny whitefly building nest-like waxy structures on leaf undersides. Unusual nesting behavior among whiteflies. On citrus and ornamentals.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest. Aleyrodidae.

desc: Small leafhopper with diagnostic paired dark spots on vertex. On various plants. Can vector aster yellows and other phytoplasma diseases to crops and ornamentals. Important as a disease vector despite small size and inconspicuous appearance.
hp: On Asteraceae (composites) — daisies, sunflowers, sagebrush. Ant associate.

desc: False indigo plant bug — small mirid specifically associated with false indigo (Amorpha californica). A native bug on a native plant — part of the evolved community of insects dependent on California's endemic shrubs. Host-specific — not found on other plants.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Miridae.

desc: Buckwheat plant bug — small mirid associated with buckwheat (Eriogonum). Host-specific. Important component of buckwheat insect community.
hp: On california buckwheat (eriogonum)

desc: Small leafhopper with diagnostic vermiculate (worm-like, irregular wavy) markings on forewings. On oaks. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community — oak trees in LA support dozens of leafhopper species, each with different feeding niches.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Family Cicadellidae. Part of the diverse canopy leafhopper community .

desc: Medium-large cicada. Males produce loud buzzing song. One of the more impressive California cicadas.
hp: Root-feeding nymph underground for years. Adult male produces loud buzzing song from trees.

desc: Small mottled leafhopper. Vectors phytoplasma diseases of various crops and ornamentals.
hp: Variegated leafhopper — vectors aster yellows and other phytoplasma diseases to crops and ornamentals. Important agricultural disease vector.

desc: Medium cicada with reddish wing veins suggesting 'wounds.' Males sing loudly from shrubs. Foothill chaparral.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Root-feeding nymph underground. Family Cicadidae. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Brilliant plant bug — small shiny predatory mirid. Important predator of aphids and mites. Beneficial in gardens.
hp: Aphid predator — important biocontrol agent. Beneficial in gardens. Preys on aphid. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Small green mirid common on willows and other riparian trees. Named for the angular shape of the cuneus (wing tip). Feeds on plant sap. Native. Common in riparian habitats throughout LA County. Part of the dominant willow-associated insect community.
hp: On willows (salix) in riparian habitat. Ant associate.

desc: Large scutellerid with shield covering entire back. On grasses. Impressive full-body shield shape.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Scutelleridae.

desc: Small cicada that produces clicking sounds by wing-clapping rather than typical cicada buzzing. Unusual sound production.

fm.Note: Clicks wings instead of buzzing — unusual

hp: Root-feeding nymph underground. Adult produces clicking sounds by wing-clapping, not tymbal vibration.

desc: Frontal planthopper — tiny delphacid on grasses. Part of the species-rich but poorly known grass-feeding planthopper community. Hundreds of species feed on grasses but are too small for casual observation. Important in grassland food webs as prey for spiders and birds.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Important prey for vertebrates.

desc: Spiny purshia psyllid — tiny psyllid on native shrubs. Part of the diverse California psyllid fauna — the state harbors hundreds of psyllid species, many undescribed, each typically associated with a specific host plant genus. Contributes to native plant-insect community dynamics.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Spiny/armed.

desc: Aquatic bug swimming upside-down, using long hind legs as oars. Air bubble on ventral surface provides buoyancy and oxygen supply. Predatory — catches mosquito larvae, small tadpoles, and aquatic insects. Can bite painfully if handled carelessly.
hp: Predator of mosquito larvae — natural mosquito biocontrol. Mosquito associate. Predatory.

desc: Green mirid specific to ceanothus (California lilac). Native bug on a native shrub. Part of diverse ceanothus insect community.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. Miridae.

desc: Tiny whitefly on native trees. Part of the diverse California whitefly fauna — dozens of native species, each typically host-specific. Distinguished from pest whiteflies by host plant (native trees, not crops). A component of native plant-insect food webs.
hp: Ecological role from description context

desc: Briggs's barklouse — small psocid living gregariously on tree bark. Feeds on algae, lichens, and fungal spores growing on bark surfaces. Not parasitic — a harmless decomposer. Lives in herds of dozens on tree trunks. Introduced from Europe. Harmless to trees.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. Parasitoid.

desc: Small psocid with diagnostic single dark wing spot. On tree bark in herds. Feeds on bark microflora — algae, fungal spores, and lichen fragments. Harmless to trees. The single wing spot distinguishes it from other bark-dwelling psocids.
hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Under bark. Single-spotted.

desc: Glass-winged barklouse — small psocid with clear (hyaline), transparent wings. On tree bark feeding on algae, lichens, and fungal growth. The transparent wings make it nearly invisible on bark. Gregarious — forms small herds. European introduction. Harmless decomposer.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. Decomposer.

desc: Medium assassin bug with crown-like spines on pronotum. Important predator of pest caterpillars and beetles. Can bite if handled.
hp: Pine; caterpillar predator. Preys on caterpillars.

desc: Distinctive seed bug with extremely elongated head. Fast runner. Under debris. Unique long head is diagnostic.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Tiny ambush bug hiding in manzanita flowers. Raptorial forelegs grab prey much larger than itself.
hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist

desc: Medium predatory bug. Important biocontrol agent — catches aphids, caterpillars, and other pests. Slender raptorial forelegs.
hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Preys on aphid. Predatory. Common.

desc: Tiny psyllid on pear trees. Major pest causing pear decline disease. Honeydew production attracts sooty mold. Important orchard pest.
hp: Plant/animal disease vector. Agricultural/garden pest.

desc: Small pale leafhopper introduced from Asia. On Japanese maples and other ornamentals. Can cause stippling.
hp: On Japanese maples and other Acer. Introduced from Asia. Can damage ornamental maples.

desc: Tiny bug that walks on water surface film. Semi-aquatic. Predatory on mosquito larvae and small insects trapped in surface tension.
hp: Predator of mosquito larvae — natural mosquito biocontrol. Mosquito associate. Predatory.

desc: Small dark seed bug with pale chevron markings. Under debris on ground. Feeds on fallen seeds. Dry habitats.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter

desc: Pale green aphid with dark knee spots (diagnostic). On potatoes, lettuce, foxglove, and many plants. Vectors plant viruses.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On potato.

desc: Small leafhopper with refined pale markings on brown body. On various shrubs in chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Part of the large genus Scaphytopius — a primarily New World leafhopper genus. Can vector phytoplasma diseases of native plants.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. In chaparral habitat. Large species.

desc: Small mirid with distinctive reddish-brown tinge. On various native plants. Part of the neotropical mirid radiation in western NA.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Family Miridae.

desc: Green aphid causing severe leaf curling on plum trees. Migrates to composites in summer. Can distort plum shoots.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Associated with Composites.

desc: Tiny psyllid introduced from Australia with its host plant hakea. On ornamental Hakea plantings in LA gardens. Causes leaf curling and stunting. An example of a specialist herbivore following its host plant into cultivation. Not established on native plants.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Dark hemispherical scale with H-shaped ridge on back. On olive, citrus, and ornamentals. Important olive pest. H-mark diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark; H-shaped ridge on back — diagnostic

hp: Black scale — major citrus and olive pest. Hemispherical black scale on twigs. Honeydew and sooty mold. Biocontrol by Metaphycus helvolus.

desc: Small dark mirid specialized on yucca plants. Can cause stippling damage (tiny pale feeding scars) on yucca leaves. Native bug on a native plant — an expected component of the yucca insect community. Named 'valida' (strong) for robust body.
hp: Specialist on yucca (yucca spp.). Ant associate.

desc: Green sharpshooter associated with yucca. Part of the diverse sharpshooter fauna. Can vector Xylella.
hp: Specialist on yucca (yucca spp.). Cicadellidae.

desc: Beautiful plant bug — tiny predatory mirid. Despite its small size, an important biocontrol agent — feeds on whiteflies, spider mites, and other small pest arthropods. Both an herbivore and predator (omnivorous mirid). Commercially considered for greenhouse biocontrol.
hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Ant associate. Spider associate.

desc: Tiny tingid with delicate lace-patterned wings, specific to yerba santa (Eriodictyon). Host-specific — one of many native lace bugs each tied to a particular plant genus. The exquisite wing venation creates a true lace pattern visible only under magnification.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Thick-leaved Yerba Santa.

desc: Small dark leafhopper with diagnostic rugose (wrinkled) forewing surface texture. Nocturnal — comes to lights. On oaks. The wrinkled wing surface is unusual among leafhoppers, which typically have smooth wings.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak).

desc: Sand-dwelling seed bug — small dark seed bug introduced from Europe. Under debris in sandy habitats. Expanding in SoCal.
hp: LA County true bug — habitat-specific ecological role. Introduced species.

desc: Tiny whitefly on mulberry trees. Can cause sooty mold from honeydew production. On ornamental mulberries.
hp: Specialist on mulberry (Morus)

desc: Tiny whitefly on manzanita (Arctostaphylos) leaf undersides. Native species on a native host. Produces waxy filaments.
hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist

desc: Woolly aphid causing severe leaf curling on ash trees. White waxy secretion. Can distort and drop ash leaves.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. Associated with Ash.

desc: California psyllid — tiny jumping plant louse on native plants. Part of the diverse California psyllid fauna — the state hosts an extraordinary radiation of native psyllids, many still undescribed. Host-plant specific. Distinguished from pest psyllids by native plant association.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Ant associate.

desc: Tiny psyllid on flannelbush (Fremontodendron). Native specialist on a native shrub. Forms galls on leaves.
hp: Gall-inducer on host plant

desc: Tiny whitefly producing diagnostic gelatinous, glass-like waxy coverings on leaf undersides. On various trees. Distinguished from other whiteflies by the transparent, jelly-like pupal case. Part of LA's diverse native whitefly assemblage.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Aleyrodidae.

desc: White waxy mealybug on bougainvillea and other ornamentals. Can cause leaf distortion and drop. Honeydew attracts sooty mold.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Bougainvillea.

desc: Small mirid on native vegetation. Named for Mexican entomologist. Part of the diverse neotropical mirid fauna reaching into SoCal.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Family Miridae.

desc: Tiny lace bug with pronounced pronotal spines. On various shrubs. Creates pale stippling on leaf upper surface from feeding.
hp: In conifer forests (pinus). Associated with Coulter Pine. Spiny/armed.

desc: Small green mirid on ceanothus (California lilac). Named for Edward O. Essig, pioneering California entomologist at UC Berkeley. One of several Ceanothia species — a genus of mirids endemic to California, each associated with ceanothus species.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. Miridae.

desc: Scale insect producing lac (resin) on creosote bush stems. Native. Creates conspicuous dark encrustations on creosote branches.
hp: Specialist on creosote bush (larrea tridentata)

desc: Very small (3mm) leafhopper, among the smallest in LA County. On various plants. Despite tiny size, can vector phytoplasma diseases. Part of the extremely species-rich leafhopper family Cicadellidae — over 22,000 species worldwide.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Cicadellidae.

desc: Small leafhopper with attractive golden streaks on pale green body. On birch and alder. The golden markings are distinctive — most leafhoppers in the canopy community are plain green or brown. Part of the birch-associated insect community.
hp: On alder (alnus) in riparian habitat. Associated with White Alder. Cicadellidae.

desc: Mirid with fine reddish markings on wings ('rufoscriptus' = red-written). On oaks and other trees. Partly predatory.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Associated with Oak.

desc: The smallest of the Ceanothia plant bugs, barely 3mm. On ceanothus (California lilac). Ceanothia is a California-endemic mirid genus — found nowhere else on Earth — with each species associated with specific Ceanothus species. A micro-endemic specialist.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. Family Miridae (3mm).

desc: Tiny (1-2mm) wingless psocid found indoors in damp conditions. Feeds on mold, fungal spores, and starchy materials including book bindings. Not a true louse — does not bite humans. Indicator of excess indoor humidity. Can damage stored documents.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Trogiidae.

desc: Dark aphid forming dense colonies on broad beans, chard, and ornamentals. Major garden pest. Complex host-alternating life cycle.
hp: Legume associate — on beans, peas, or other Fabaceae

desc: Small psocid found on tree bark. Feeds on microflora — algae, lichens, and fungal spores growing on bark surfaces.
hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Under bark. Barbara's.

desc: Larger than most psocids. On tree bark feeding on algae and lichen microflora. One of the more conspicuous barklice.
hp: Feeds on lichens and bark microflora. Under bark. Large species.

desc: Large bark-colored stink bug. Feeds on tree fruits. Bark-cryptic when resting on tree trunks. Rough textured.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Under bark.

desc: Ectoparasite of bats related to bed bugs. In bat roosts. Can temporarily bite humans when bats are excluded from buildings.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Parasitoid. Western.

desc: Dark aphid on waterlilies and aquatic plants. One of few truly aquatic aphids. Can damage ornamental pond plants.

fm.Color: Dark; on waterlilies and aquatic plants

hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Preys on aphid.

desc: Tiny bug with intricate lace wing pattern on oak leaf undersides. Causes stippling damage. Beautiful under magnification.

fm.Color: White lace pattern; on oak leaves

hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Very small species.

desc: Small pale rhopalid with darker dorsal stripe. On various weedy plants. Unlike stink bugs, lacks strong defensive odor.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Rhopalidae.

desc: Pale-bordered stink bug — medium green-brown pentatomid with distinctive pale lateral edges. On various plants — generalist feeder. Distinguished from Euschistus by paler margins and slightly different scutellum shape. Common but frequently misidentified. Native.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Pentatomidae.

desc: Small dark rhyparochromid with partial pale crossband on forewings. Under bark and debris. Feeds on fallen seeds and detritus. Predatory on small invertebrates as well. Litter-dwelling community. Nocturnal. Native to western NA.
hp: Ground-dwelling seed feeder — on fallen seeds under litter

desc: Small green pentatomid. 'Calva' (bald) refers to the smooth, unornamented scutellum. Feeds on fruit and developing seeds of various plants. Generalist herbivore. Distinguished from other green stink bugs by the smooth, unpunctured scutellar surface.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Pentatomidae.

desc: Antigua plant bug — small dark mirid introduced from Central America, now established in SoCal gardens. On various ornamental plants. Part of the growing list of Neotropical mirid introductions reaching LA — likely via nursery trade in ornamental plants.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Family Miridae. Small dark mirid introduced from Central America, now establ.

desc: Small metallic blue predatory stink bug. Feeds on flea beetles and other chrysomelids. Beneficial. One of the most colorful pentatomids.

fm.Color: Metallic blue; predatory on flea beetles

hp: On beets, chard, or spinach (Chenopodiaceae/Amaranthaceae). Metallic. Small metallic blue predatory stink bug

desc: Blackberry-cereal aphid — green aphid on cereals and grasses, also colonizing Rubus (blackberry). Important crop pest capable of transmitting barley yellow dwarf virus. Alternate hosts include strawberry and grasses. Part of the economically important grain aphid complex.
hp: Aphid alternating between Rubus (blackberry) and cereal grasses. Crop virus vector.

desc: Coreopsis aphid — small dark aphid on coreopsis and related composites. Can build dense colonies on flower stems.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers

desc: Soft scale insect on citrus, ficus, and many ornamentals. Produces copious honeydew. Major nursery pest.
hp: Sap feeder on Citrus. Produces honeydew promoting sooty mold. Agricultural pest

desc: Pale green soft scale on coffee, citrus, and ornamentals. Tropical origin. Produces honeydew.
hp: Green scale — soft scale on citrus, coffee, ornamentals. Produces copious honeydew promoting sooty mold. Important in tropical agriculture.

desc: Soft scale producing white cottony egg sac. On guava, citrus, and many ornamentals. Tropical pest established in SoCal.
hp: Green shield scale — on citrus and ornamentals. Produces white cottony egg sac beneath body. Honeydew attracts ants and promotes sooty mold.

desc: Green aphid alternating between dogwood and milkweed. Complex seasonal host-switching life cycle.
hp: On milkweed (asclepias) — aposematic

desc: Small mirid on native vegetation in chaparral. Part of the diverse but poorly studied native plant bug fauna.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. Family Miridae. Small mirid on native vegetation in chaparral. Part of the d.

desc: Very small, fragile mirid of coastal sage scrub and chaparral. On native Asteraceae. Feeds on plant sap and small arthropods. Part of a primarily Neotropical genus represented in LA by this single delicate species at its northern range limit.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage

desc: Branching plant bug — small green-brown mirid on various trees and shrubs. Part of the enormous genus Phytocoris — over 500 species worldwide. Most are nocturnal and come to lights. Both herbivorous and predatory — feeds on plant sap and small arthropods.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs.

desc: Green aphid alternating between currant (winter) and sowthistle (summer). Vectors lettuce necrotic yellows virus.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On lettuce.

desc: Brown convex scale on many fruit and ornamental trees. Introduced from Europe. Produces honeydew attracting sooty mold.
hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. Introduced species.

desc: Tiny pale aphid with dark spots on bamboo. Introduced from Asia. Can coat bamboo leaves with honeydew.

fm.Color: Pale with dark spots; on bamboo

hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. Preys on aphid. Introduced species.

desc: Tiny yellow aphid with dark dorsal spots. Major pest of alfalfa — can devastate stands. Injects toxic saliva.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On alfalfa.

desc: Small mirid active at dusk. Named for collector Paddock. On various plants in chaparral. Part of the poorly known evening-active mirid fauna.
hp: Native chaparral scrub habitat. In chaparral habitat. Paddock's.

desc: Small green mirid endemic to California. On native shrubs in chaparral and coastal sage scrub. Feeds on plant sap. Part of the California-endemic component of the mirid fauna — several genera are found nowhere else. Conservation-relevant by endemism.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. In chaparral habitat. California.

desc: Behrens's treehopper — small brown membracid on oaks. Named for James Behrens, early California naturalist. Distinctive pronotal 'horn' — treehoppers are famous for extravagant pronotal sculptures, though this species is relatively modest. On twigs. Honeydew attracts ants.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Behrens'.

desc: Blood-feeding bug living in cliff swallow nests. Related to bed bugs. Can enter homes when swallows nest on buildings.
hp: Obligate human blood parasite. Nocturnal feeder hiding in mattress seams.

desc: Aphid on white sage (Artemisia ludoviciana) and related sagebrush. Native aphid on native plants.
hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with California Sagebrush

desc: Green aphid on willows. Native species. Part of the diverse California aphid fauna — the state hosts hundreds of native aphid species, each typically host-specific. Distinguished from introduced aphids by native host association.
hp: On willows (salix) in riparian habitat. Introduced species.

desc: Tiny psyllid with distinctive reddish-orange coloring. On native shrubs. One of the more colorful psyllid species.
hp: Pear psyllid — NOT a blood feeder (mislabeled). Tiny sap-feeding psyllid on Pyrus (pear). Can vector pear decline phytoplasma.

desc: Green aphid on wheat, barley, and other cereals. Major crop pest. Vectors barley yellow dwarf virus.
hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. On wheat.

desc: Small green froghopper. Nymphs produce frothy spittle masses on plant stems. Adults jump powerfully.
hp: Nymphs produce frothy spittle masses on plant stems — protective foam shelters. Adults free-living.

desc: Small scutellerid with highly variable coloring and pattern (hence 'proteus,' the shape-shifting Greek god). Shield covers entire abdomen. On grasses. Distinguished from stink bugs by the greatly enlarged scutellum covering the entire dorsum.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Scutelleridae.

desc: Small dark saldid found at water margins. Predatory. Jumps and flies short distances when disturbed.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Saldidae.

desc: Broom aphid — aphid on broom (Cytisus, Genista) and related legumes. Introduced from Europe with ornamental broom plantings. Can cause shoot distortion and honeydew accumulation on ornamental shrubs. Part of the large influx of European aphids following ornamental plant trade.
hp: Colonial sap feeder producing honeydew. Associated with Legumes.

desc: Large red-and-black assassin bug of desert habitats. Ambushes pollinators on flowers. Related to California bee assassin.
hp: Flower-ambush predator — captures pollinating insects on flower heads.

desc: Small mirid associated with sagebrush (Artemisia) and related Asteraceae shrubs. Host-specific. Chaparral habitat.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with California Sagebrush.

desc: Small stink bug with black and reddish-orange banding. On various plants. Related to C. conspicillaris.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Black-banded.

desc: Tiny red-colored scale insect on date palm fronds. Can damage date production. Named for entomologist C.L. Marlatt.
hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect. Scale insect associate.

desc: Small mirid with darker dorsal markings. On various plants. Both herbivorous and predatory on small arthropods.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Miridae.

desc: Dark aphid with long cornicles on guava and related plants. Introduced from Asia. On ornamental Myrtaceae.
hp: Aphid predator. Important biocontrol agent. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On corn.

desc: Green glossy aphid on Artemisia (sagebrush/tarragon). Unusually shiny for an aphid.
hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with California Sagebrush

desc: Small dark brown membracid resembling a thorn on a twig. The dark coloring suggests a tuxedo (hence the common name). On various trees and shrubs. Honeydew production attracts ants that protect the treehopper from predators in exchange.
hp: Predatory hemipteran — captures other insects with raptorial forelegs. Membracidae.

desc: Small pale green acanaloniid. Broad flat body resembles a leaf fragment. On various trees and shrubs.
hp: Sap feeder on shrubs. Broad flat body mimics a leaf fragment. Produces flocculent wax.

desc: Tiny planthopper on grasses. Part of the diverse but poorly known Delphacidae — one of the largest families of planthoppers, with hundreds of grass-feeding species. Too small for casual observation but collectively important herbivores in grassland ecosystems.
hp: Grass sap-feeder (Poaceae). Part of grassland herbivore community. On Poaceae (grasses). Grassland habitat.

desc: Small dark seed bug under debris. Named for Swedish entomologist Carl Boheman. Feeds on fallen seeds in dry habitats.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Rhyparochromidae.

desc: Tiny bright green psyllid on various plants. Can cause leaf curling. Part of the diverse psyllid fauna of western NA.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Triozidae.

desc: Intermediate plant bug — small green mirid of intermediate size within its genus. On various shrubs. Feeds on plant sap.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Ant associate.

desc: Small, quick-running mirid. Both herbivorous (sap-feeding) and predatory on mites, aphids, and whitefly eggs. Important biocontrol agent in tomato greenhouses — commercially released in Europe for whitefly management. On solanaceous plants in LA gardens.
hp: Biocontrol agent; aphid predator. Specialist aphid predator — important biocontrol. On tomato.

desc: Small pale leafhopper. One of the more poetically named insects in the guide. On various plants in chaparral. The genus Neocoelidia is primarily New World. Distinguished by male genitalia — like many leafhoppers, external characters alone cannot confirm identity.
hp: Chaparral sap feeder — on native shrubs. Part of native plant-insect community. Family Cicadellidae. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Tiny treehopper with zigzag horn on pronotum. On oaks. Part of the diverse treehopper fauna.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Very small species.

desc: Small dark rhyparochromid found under debris in dry habitats. Feeds on fallen seeds of native plants. Named for P.W. Oman, prominent 20th-century hemipterist. Part of the diverse but poorly known ground-dwelling seed bug community in California chaparral.
hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Chaparral habitat. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Small green mirid. Part of the enormous family Miridae — the largest family of Heteroptera with over 11,000 described species. Feeds on plant sap. On native chaparral vegetation. Most mirids are too small and cryptic for non-specialists to notice.
hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue. Chaparral

desc: Scale insect with white waxy plates arranged like flags/ensigns. On saltbush (Atriplex). Native.
hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect. Scale insect associate.

desc: Small dark cydnid with bumpy legs. Lives in soil around plant roots. Feeds on root sap.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Small black lygaeid under debris in dry habitats. Feeds on fallen seeds. Distinguished from other dark seed bugs by uniformly jet-black coloring. In coastal sage scrub and chaparral — under rocks and leaf litter in arid microhabitats.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Small mirid with bold dark markings on pale green body. On native Asteraceae and other plants in coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Plant sap feeder. Part of the enormous family Miridae — over 11,000 species worldwide, the largest family of true bugs.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage

desc: Pale rhopalid with distinctive reddish wing veins (hence 'rubrovenosus'). On various plants in dry habitats. Scentless plant bugs (Rhopalidae) resemble small coreids but lack the scent glands. Feeds on seeds and developing fruit.
hp: Plant sap feeder on various hosts. Ant associate.

desc: Cottony scale on elms producing white waxy secretion. Introduced from Europe. Can weaken elm trees.

fm.Color: Brown with white waxy fringe; on elms

hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. On cotton.

desc: Small psocid on tree bark. Named for pioneering dipterist Daniel W. Coquillett of the USDA. Feeds on bark microflora.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Under bark. Coquillett's.

desc: Circular armored scale on manzanita (Arctostaphylos) bark. White waxy cover protects the sessile female underneath. Native species on a native host — part of the naturally occurring insect community on chaparral shrubs. Not a pest in natural settings.

fm.Color: White waxy cover on manzanita bark

hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. In chaparral habitat.

desc: White armored scale on cycad leaves. Can damage ornamental cycads and sagos. Introduced. White waxy covers on fronds.
hp: Sessile sap feeder — honeydew producer. Introduced species.

desc: Tiny armored scale on oak bark and twigs. Circular white waxy cover. Native species on native oaks. Usually not damaging.

fm.Color: Tiny white circular; on oak bark

hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. Under bark. Very small species.

desc: White elongated armored scale on pine needles. Can cause yellowing and needle drop. White waxy covers conspicuous on green needles.

fm.Color: White elongated waxy cover on pine needles

hp: Sap-feeding hemipteran on host plant tissue
